full_response:
Step 1 –  Formalizing the “no–overlap” condition  
Every router covers the closed disk of radius 1 centred at its position.  
For two routers i and j with centres pᵢ , pⱼ,

 their coverages overlap  ⇔  dist(pᵢ , pⱼ) < 2.

(If the distance were ≥ 2 the disks would be disjoint or meet in at most one boundary point, which causes no interior overlap.)

Hence we look for the largest subset of the given four points in which every pair of points is at distance ≥ 2.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2 –  Compute all pairwise distances
Let  

A = (0,0)  B = (1.2,0)  C = (0,1.2)  D = (0.4,0.4).

• AB = √((1.2−0)²+(0−0)²) = 1.2  
• AC = √((0−0)²+(1.2−0)²) = 1.2  
• AD = √((0.4−0)²+(0.4−0)²) = √0.32 ≈ 0.566  
• BC = √((1.2−0)²+(0−1.2)²) = √2.88 ≈ 1.697  
• BD = √((1.2−0.4)²+(0−0.4)²) = √0.8  ≈ 0.894  
• CD = √((0−0.4)²+(1.2−0.4)²) = √0.8  ≈ 0.894  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3 –  Compare with the threshold 2
All six distances are strictly less than 2.  
Therefore every pair of routers overlaps.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 4 –  Deduce the maximum size of a feasible set
Because any two routers interfere, no feasible set can contain more than one router.  
Conversely, a single router obviously causes no conflict, so size 1 is attainable.

Hence the maximum number of routers that can remain on is exactly 1.

\boxed{\text{A. }1}

Confidence: 93%
